[RISH SONG BOOKS IN THE ROYAL IRISH ACADEMY.

Read beforo the Academy on 27th January, 1906, and now printed by
kind permission of the Council.

For the purpose of drawing attention to the recently arranged
Song Books, I venture to offer the following general remarks upon
them. I may say here that the British Museum is the only place I know
of with a like collection.

These Song Books (which are quite distinct from, though closely
akin o, chap books) are now bound in six 12mo volumes of varying
bulk. They had been originally in two separate bundles or collections
and lay 1n two different places. Attention was first drawn to one
collection only and, with the approval of the Council, it was arranged
and bound in four volumes. This had largely been done when the
Librarian in the course of his cataloguing the MSS. in the Strong
Room, came upon the second bundle or collection. which had then to be
‘arranged and bound separately in two volumes. This was unavoidable,

il Sl o e T g Dl o
. . T

but unfortunate as it rendered the first arrangement incomplete.
The main principles of arrangement have been first territorial and
I_then chronological, or in other words, the Song Books have been first
| Erouped according to the places at which they were prmted or pub-
'_ lished, and then ‘those of each place are put in order of date, if date

S While the single sheev ballad is still fairly common, the Song

Book has disappeared. It qonsisted of four leaves or cight Pages

e N R N T R (S ST T M N S L T Y

g There was usually a very rude woodcut in the centre of the firgt o
: title-page. The name of each song (or ballad) was given at the top E‘
and the imprint below. This latter varied much. Sometimes place, I

printer and date were all given, but often only two of these apg

occasionally, none. However, where the printer’s name alone is giyen
the place and approximate date can almost certainly be determined.
The paper 1s generally coarse, the type, bad, old and worn out,

T i — gy

and the printung wretched and full of errors, but still this is not

always so, as for example those printed by Kelly in Waterford. |
It must be remembered, too, that most of these Song Books in i
E this collection have been much han&led and soiled through use. Itis
1 very desirable that a full index of the whole collection he made soon.
b Until this is done the exact contents cannot be made use of, nor its
' true value estimated. |
E The contents are very varied. A few are entirely religious, cor
| i sisting of Hymns or Prayers. Several deal with local events, such as
1its hoat or shipwrecks, murders, hangings, etc. Others are in praise o
] some local hero or beauty, or of some particular place. Many are love |
dittis, or laments) of forsaken swains or sweethearvs.  Events o
, public or national interest form occasionally the subject of these bﬂ]"
RVt - lads as for example the Union, vhe Battle of Waterloo, and Cﬂ";’hom
Emancipation. Purely English hallads or ditties occur several t1mes ,-
a3 “ Jervis taking the Spanish Fleet,” ¢ Black-eyed Susan,” uf}omj |
i live with me,” ‘‘ The Bonnetts so Blue,” ‘‘ God Save the Klng’”
< Highland Mary,” * Tibby Fowler,”  The Hunting of Chevy Ob®
« Nae Luck about the House.”” Nor are the local songs merely 'te.

g Another point worth noticing is, that the place of publication has
5. an influence on the character of the songs. For instance those printed
0 *’;ﬁ in Belfast contain, as one might expect, ballads in praise of ‘the Orange
P ' Society and 1ts principles and heroes, and also more Scotch and
& | English ballads. ’
1 t The woodcuts deserve examination. They are often repeated and
m may help in fixing dates or places or printers.

[l Ephemeral as these Song Books are it cannot be a matter of sur-
t, ‘ prise that comparatively few survive. Rather it is surprising that so
ot i many: remain.

n | large number of them and has had them bound, but nov so carefu]ly .

ts copies, lacking one or more leaves, which may hereafter perhaps be

completed or help to complete other copies. There are, among these | -

- Scraps, several first leaves or title-pages which are of some value.
18 There are, it should be mentioned, a few numbers in prose, such i
of s “The Wandering Jew,” ‘“ The Divine Revelation,” etc., which

EARLIEST PRINTING IN ATHLONE.

Some years ago I wrote an article on Athlone printing ip th
eighteenth century for the ‘‘ Dublin Penny Journal,” which wag nlm:
in existence, and issued from the office of the old “ Nation,” Since
then I have gained some further particulars, and therefore I think therq
1s justification for repeating the list, with all additions I have recelyed
up to the present in I.B.L., which will come before a different cirels
of readers.

There is not much to record regarding printing in Athlone, for
~vhat there is, consists almost entirely of two newspapers, the * At}
Ione Chronicle ’ and the ‘‘ Athlone Herald.” I do not know certamly
who printed the ‘‘ Athlone Chronicle,”” but presumably it was the pr(;.
prietor and editor, Ephraim Proctor. The printer of the ‘‘ Athlone
Herald ’* was Denis Daly, who continued to print it for several years,
and 1t 18 regrettable that only six numbers of Tt are extant and that
they are scattered in three different places. Tt continued down to
1824, when it was printed by Daniel Daly, presumably a son or other
relative of Denis who printed the earlier numbers.

It i1s right to mention that Madden states there was also a third
early newspaper published at Athlone, namely, the ‘‘ Athlone
Sentinel ’ of which John Daly is given as the printer, but no copy s
extant. I am rather inclined to think that there is some confusion
here. In the British Museum there is a mnewspaper called the
‘“ Athlone Sentinel,”” but it did not begin until the year 1834. I hope
some of our readers can clear up this point, and add something to this
short list, which gives such very meagre particulars of A'hlono
printing.

1770  The Athlone Chronicle? [See—1788].

name is still in the Law List for 1910. His musical compositions

86 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.
I used to meet him 1n the sixties at a friend’s Wwha
" ! - | myoa o . . | rB
his  great musical abilities made him conspicuous, |yt I

suppose our paths were different so that I never saw hin after
the seventies. But when writing my ‘“ Swimming,”” 1904, I w anteq t,
make use of some of the matter in his “ Our Autumn Holiday,” 4
wrote to him through his publishers. He replied from ‘¢ Woollay's »
hesitating about giving his consent, as ha was intending to publish the
parts I wanted. I then told him that I already had the permission of
Mr. Linley Sambourne and Messrs. Agnew, the publishers and proprie.
tors of the copyright, and that I had written to him as a matter of
courtesy, but nevertheless I would not use the matter if he objected,.
Not long after he sent his consent, but I do not find that he published
another book.

He seems never to have got over the usual Irish modesty of character
though he spent the whole of his life in England. My evidence of this
is the very modest account given of him in ‘“ Who’s Who,”” a proof of
which I presume he saw. That his name never got in until after his
death is one of those strange things it is difficult to account for, con-
sidering the numbers of young men whose names are in who have never
done more than write a few novels, certainly the said young mens’ books
are not known all over the world like Molloy’s worKs. The motice In
‘“ Who’s Who ’’ only occupies seven lines and only mentions four
songs! Another extraordinary fact is that I am unable to find any
notice of his death in a single English law paper. His name is nob 11
Grove’s Dictionary of Music, but Dr. Riemann in his dictionary gives
him a few lines. Molloy first appears in ‘‘ Who’s Who )7 the year aftel
his death as he died at his residence (?) Woolley’s Hambledon, Bucks
dn the 4th, and his will was proved on the 27th February, 1909. His
occupy
twenty pages of the catalogue of the British Museum Library, the ﬁ“'t
date is 1865, the last 1904. ‘Besides these he wrote * Our Autumn Holr
day on French Rivers,”” 1874, an edition was published at Boston, Mast
in 1879. Tt is in respect of this interesting work as I have already oo

| 08

RICOLLECTIONS OF AN OLD BOOK LOVER.

n the year 1860, I read, with great interest, “under Prof. Ormsby,

.t the Catholic University, Dublin, *‘ The Greek Testament. From Card.
Mai's Ed. of the Catholic Bible, with notes, chiefly Philological and
Exegetical. A Harmony of the Gospels.”” By Robt. Ormsby, M.A. 8vo.,
published by Duffey, and printed by J. M. O’Toole, Dublin, 1860.
Wo students who loved Card. Newman, expected in the fifties, a
book of just this kind from him. He left Dublin and the Catholic Um-
versity, if I remember rightly, in 1858. This elaborate edition with let-
ter of Approbation from Card. Cullen, 1ts learned preface, its parallels
and numerous long appendixes and text—in all extends to 612 pages
and 2,472 notes. I was a teacher by day, but a student by night dur-
during the whole of the fifties, and found it hard to steal a night for
Dickens’ readings in the Rotunda. 1 often - wondered why Prof.
Ormsby published this fine edition of Greek Testament if he knew that
his dear friend, Card. Newman, would soon publish his, and become his
dear friend’s rival? I formed the opinion thav the exegetical mnotes
wore Newman’s. I did not read the reviews of the book, but read be-
bween the lines in forming this opinion, while reading the book under
Ormsby. This very interesting book was published atb 10s. 6d., but
never reprinted. If I am right in my opinion, the most learned work of
our beloved rector is neglected and in fact is a rare book now. In the

Sixties I wag professor of mathematics in the High School, Harcourt
Strogt, and ¢ coached many T.C.D. students. When Mr. Lecky stood
Tor Trinity, I canvassed his friends by letter, but he found it out, and .
“T0to to thank me. In Parliament and out of it, he remained my

Ttond and correspondent, chrough lifé, ‘and strove to obtain a charter Sares A

for thﬁ Cabhahc Unwermty The new Umvers:t’ov wﬂl in fu‘bureg ?' |

THE WHITLEY STOKES LIBRARY.

There was an interesting assemblage at TUniversity Collegs
Gower Street, London, on Friday, 9th December, when the Presentatig,
was formally made of the library formed by the late Dr. Whitley
Stokes to that institution. The library consists of about 3,500
volumes and pamphlets, many of which are presentation copieg from
the writers to Dr. Stokes, and contain valuable notes and additiong
either by him or by the authors. About one-third of the books ang
most of the pamphlets bear exclusively upon Celtic studies,, while the
greater part of the remainder of the library consists of works of phil
logy and folklore. The main strength of the library lies in the col-
lection of martyrologies, lives of saints, and other works of early Irish
religious literature. A large proportion of these Irish martyrologies
was edited by Dr. Stokes himself. It is in his MS. annotations that
the extraordinary importance and interest of the library to Celtic
scholarship consists.

Lkl T included Miss Maive and Miss Annie Stokes, the donors of their father’s
j‘ library to the College. Speeches were delivered eulogising the work
t of Dr. Stokes for the Celtic language, literature, and philology, an
© welcoming the gift of his library. Sir Charles Lyall, in making th°

o F . presentation, said it was fitting that Dr. Stokes’ library should be
| 3;-4 s ~ given to University College, as that College was the only jnstitution

~  in London where provision had been made for giving instruction of ¥

University character in the Celtic language. Dr. Kuno Meyer 5‘“‘:,_

B'h manuscripts. Were it not for his labours in this neglected old
i R 711 would not have been m a posltmn to judge the extent OF *‘h”

n 1883, the late Lord Russell of Killowen visited America 1n the
company of Lord Chief Justice Coleridge. He kept a journal during
his vigit, and 1t has now been published in America under the ftitle,
« Diary of a Visiv to the United States.” The contents of the diary
are characterised by all Lord Russell’s shrewdness of observation and
gifts of expression. While he was in New York he called upon the widow
and son of John Mitchel, which distinguished Irishman he had known in
reland. ** I still think him,” he says of Mitchel, in this diary, ‘‘ the
most brilliant journalistic writer I have ever known. A portion of this
diary was printed by Barry O’Brien in his *‘ Life of Lord Russell.”’

Reviewing Mr. Frankfort Moore’s new ‘ Life of Goldsmith *’ (Con-

stable), in the ‘‘ Daily Chronicle,”” Mr. Clement Shorter, the editor of

‘“The Sphere,”’ writes:—‘¢ Of the writer of the best novel, the best
poem, and the best comedy of his age, it may be said that it is not

known for certain where he was born, nor the exact spot where he was

buried. Five out of six of the biographers we have na.med place his
birth at Pallas, County Longford. Mr. Moore mentions both Pallas

and Elphin, but gives the preference to the last-named place on the

strength of Thomas Campbell’s ‘¢ Historical Survey,’’ a book which
offers no real evidence. Not one of the six biographers seems to have
met with Isaac Weld’s ¢ Statistical Survey of the County of Roscom-
mon ”’ (Dublin, 1832), a copy of which is in my library. Here I ﬁnd'-'

well-nigh indisputable proof that Goldsmith was born at Airdnagubhan, f;

or Smith’s Hill, about half a' mile from Elphin, County Roscommon: .

pStmnge that that blessed word A,lrdnagubhan has never yet intruded
‘ﬂm]f into a bxogra,phy of the author of ‘* The Vicar of Wakefield.””

g drew Mr, Shorter’s attention to Rev. Dr. Kelly’s ‘The Early Hsmn’os

An 1nteresting discussion has been going on in the pages of «y
and Q,” relative to the first appearance of the ¢ Yellow-back * Novel
It 1s there stated on the authority of Mr. John Ward, a member of thc;
once famous publishing firm of Marcus Ward, that the inventor of the
““ Green-back ’’ novel, the predecessor of the ‘f Yellow-back,” was John
Simms of the firm of Simms and McIntyre, of Belfast, who still liyes
there and 1s now well over ninety years of age. This was the famous
old series, ‘‘ The Parlour Library,” regarding which I shall have S0me
thing interesting to say shortly.

Looking through the December Magazines, I find that Mr. T. J.
Westropp has an interesting article on ‘‘ Folk Lore of County Clare”
in ‘‘ Folk Lore ”’; Mr. John Redmond contributes one ‘‘ What Ireland
Wants,”” dealing with the political situation to ‘‘ Nash’s,”’ and Canon
Sheehan discusses ‘‘ Mr. O’Brien and the Irish Centre Party ’’ in the
‘“ Fortnightly.”’

The General Election with all its good and bad fortune, is over with
these results for Irish litterateurs:—Major Adam, poet soldier, is re
jected of Woolwich; Mr. Cooke-Taylor, a well-known member of LS.
and grandson of the historian of ‘‘ The Civil Wars in Ireland ’’ shares
a like fate in Holborn; Mr. Stephen Gwynn sits again for Galway
whilst Shane Leslie is once more defeated in Derry.

The new part of the CoUNTY LouTE ARCHZOLOGICAL JOURNAL (Tem-
pest, Dundalk), will bear comparison with any of its predecessors. The
general ‘‘ get-up " reflects the highest credit upon the printer and
publisher, the illustrations are produced in the highest style of art,
and the literary contributions are varied and excellent. The place of
honour is held by an article on ‘‘ St. Patrick and Louth,” from the
pen of Rev. Prof. Murray, of Minnesota. It occupies twenty-four
pages and is a masterpiece of research and learned argument. Mr.
Henry Morris describes a recent visit to Emania and its spoliation,
regarding which we hope shortly to print an unpublished anecdote of
Bishop Reeves. General Stubb’s articles on ‘¢ Place Names in Lout 2
we trust one day to see republished in volume form. They are well
worth it. Altogether, an excellent number.

The first volume of a new and complete edition of THE CORRESPOND-
ENCE OF JONATHAN Swirt, has just been issued by Messrs. Bell. The
publishers had originally entrusted the editing of this handsome seriess
of six volumes to the late Litton Falkiner. His sudden death was like
to have upset their arrangements, but they were fortunate enough to-
secure the services of his friend, Mr. Elrington Bqll, M,.R.I.A.,,_, whose.
mtimate acquaintance with Irish history and literature gives him
Special qualifications for a task which he has admirably performed. Mr.

Ball, whose excellent ‘* History of County Dublin,” is known to all

T readers, has discovered a very large quantity of hitherto unpub-

The Oxford University Press announces, ‘‘ Ireland under the Nor.
mans, 1169-1216,”” by Goddard H. Orpen, who so ably edited for then
in 1892, the old French poem, ‘ The Song of Dermot and the Ear],”
from the Carew MSS. in Lambeth Palace.

Early in the New Year Messrs. Putnam will issue a volume en.
titled ‘‘ Incidents in My Life,”” by Dr. Thomas Addis Emmet, grand.
son of the Thomas Emmet who was a brother of Robert Emmet, and
himself one of the leaders of the insurrectionary movement in Ireland
of 1798. We once heard Shane Leslie tell a pretty story of Dr
Emmet. Although approaching his eightieth year, he commenced the
study of Gaelic, and when asked the reason, replied, ‘‘ I think there
will ba a greatv deal of it spoken in Heaven.”’

Qur esteemed contributor, Mr. William MacArthur, is engagd
upon an important topographical work that will do for the streets,
avenues, and roads of County Dublin, what Rev. Dr. McCready has al-
teady done for the city, in his ‘‘ Dublin Street Names, Dated and ex
plained.”’ (Hodges and Figgis.) 1892,

pr. D. D. Murcany (Vol. IL., p. 58).—It was stated in some of the
New York papers at the time of his death, that Dr. Mulcahy’s library
wvas worth 10,000 dollars (£2,000.) After his death, this fine collection
of some 20,000 volumes was sold by auction. It contained a consider-
able number of books, pamphlets, manuscripts, etc., relating vo the
history, topography, and antiquities of Ireland, the majority of which
vere bought in for Mr. S. J. Richardson, Editor of the New York
« (Gael.” Amongst the items purchased by Mr. Richardson were a
copy of Don Philip O’Sullivan Beare’s History of Catholic Ireland in
Latin, described as ‘‘ a revised reprint of the 1621 Lisbon edition,
edited by Prof. Matthew Kelly, of Maynooth, and published by John
(Daly in Dublin in 1850 ”’; a MS. translation into English of a con-
siderable portion of the history just mentioned; and a MS. catalogue
in Spanish and English containing a list of historic papers and corres-
pondence relating to Ireland stored in the archives of the Castle of
Simancas, in Spain.
